blocks.text: Six days later, Jesus took with him Peter and James and John, and led them up a high mountain apart, by themselves. And he was transfigured before them,
2 : , see 5.37n. , see 3.13n. , lit., “changed in form,” a term that speaks to Jesus's divine nature (cf. 9.7; see Dan 11.36).
and his clothes became dazzling white, such as no one on earth could bleach them.
And there appeared to them Elijah with Moses, who were talking with Jesus.
4 : , individuals expected by many to play a role in God's kingdom dawning on earth (see 1.6n.).
Then Peter said to Jesus, “Rabbi, it is good for us to be here; let us make three dwellings, one for you, one for Moses, and one for Elijah.”

5 : , a Jewish title meaning “teacher” (lit., “my master”), used occasionally of Jesus. , or “tents,” temporary shelters like the shrine God inhabited before Solomon built the Temple (Ex 25.9; 2 Sam 7.2,6).
He did not know what to say, for they were terrified.
Then a cloud overshadowed them, and from the cloud there came a voice, “This is my Son, the Beloved; listen to him!”
7 : , a heavenly voice declares Jesus's divine sonship to the disciples (cf. 1.11). , a command that fits Jesus's prophet-like role (see Deut 18.15).
Suddenly when they looked around, they saw no one with them any more, but only Jesus.
As they were coming down the mountain, he ordered them to tell no one about what they had seen, until after the Son of Man had risen from the dead.
9 : Jesus's identity, for Mark, grows clear only after the resurrection. See 1.34n.
So they kept the matter to themselves, questioning what this rising from the dead could mean.
10 : , see 6.16n.; part of the Jewish apocalyptic worldview. See also Mt 25.31–46.
Then they asked him, “Why do the scribes say that Elijah must come first?”
He said to them, “Elijah is indeed coming first to restore all things. How then is it written about the Son of Man, that he is to go through many sufferings and be treated with contempt?

12 : , God's coming kingdom entailed sweeping restoration of the cosmic order. , though no known Jewish traditions claim that a figure of this name will suffer (cf. Dan 7.1–27), apocalyptic texts consistently assume a suffering destiny for those who remain righteous and/or faithful to God in the face of earthly oppressors (Dan 11.33–35; 2 Macc 7).
But I tell you that Elijah has come, and they did to him whatever they pleased, as it is written about him.”
9.2–13: Jesus's mountaintop transfiguration (Mt 17.1–8; Lk 9.28–36).
When they came to the disciples, they saw a great crowd around them, and some scribes arguing with them.
When the whole crowd saw him, they were immediately overcome with awe, and they ran forward to greet him.
He asked them, “What are you arguing about with them?”
Someone from the crowd answered him, “Teacher, I brought you my son; he has a spirit that makes him unable to speak;
and whenever it seizes him, it dashes him down; and he foams and grinds his teeth and becomes rigid; and I asked your disciples to cast it out, but they could not do so.”
18 : The details fit common symptoms of epileptic seizures. , lit., “were not strong.” Typically in Mark, the defeat of evil forces depends on strength (see 1.7; 3.27; 5.4; 14.37).
He answered them, “You faithless generation, how much longer must I be among you? How much longer must I put up with you? Bring him to me.”
19 : , see 8.12n. In this setting, the phrase may refer to Jesus's disciples, to the father, or to an unspecified group.
And they brought the boy to him. When the spirit saw him, immediately it convulsed the boy,a and he fell on the ground and rolled about, foaming at the mouth.
Jesus asked the father, “How long has this been happening to him?” And he said, “From childhood.
It has often cast him into the fire and into the water, to destroy him; but if you are able to do anything, have pity on us and help us.”
Jesus said to him, “If you are able!—All things can be done for the one who believes.”
23 : , trust in God's kingly power creates a channel for its effective restoration of well-being and wholeness (see 2.5; 5.34; 11.22–23; cf. 6.5–6).
Immediately the father of the child cried out, “I believe; help my unbelief!”
24 : , a prayer that acknowledges the father's ambiguous relationship to faith.

When Jesus saw that a crowd came running together, he rebuked the unclean spirit, saying to it, “You spirit that keeps this boy from speaking and hearing, I command you, come out of him, and never enter him again!”
After crying out and convulsing him terribly, it came out, and the boy was like a corpse, so that most of them said, “He is dead.”
But Jesus took him by the hand and lifted him up, and he was able to stand.
When he had entered the house, his disciples asked him privately, “Why could we not cast it out?”
He said to them, “This kind can come out only through prayer.”
9.14–29: Jesus heals a child who had epilepsy (Mt 17.14–20; Lk 9.37–43a).
29 : , Mark does not specify whose prayer is effective in this case, though the story mentions only the father's petition for faith (v. 24).
They went on from there and passed through Galilee. He did not want anyone to know it;
30 : On Jesus's not wanting , see 1.43–44n.
for he was teaching his disciples, saying to them, “The Son of Man is to be betrayed into human hands, and they will kill him, and three days after being killed, he will rise again.”
31 : , lit., “handed over,” a detail not included in the first prediction (8.31; cf. 14.11,18).
But they did not understand what he was saying and were afraid to ask him.
32 : Despite the repeated nature of the prediction, the disciples still fail to grasp Jesus's destiny.
Then they came to Capernaum; and when he was in the house he asked them, “What were you arguing about on the way?”
But they were silent, for on the way they had argued with one another who was the greatest.
He sat down, called the twelve, and said to them, “Whoever wants to be first must be last of all and servant of all.”
35 : , Jesus redefines the concept of greatness discussed by the disciples (9.34). Cf. 8.34–35.
Then he took a little child and put it among them; and taking it in his arms, he said to them,
36 : , an illustrative example of true greatness. The ancient world assigned little social value to children.
“Whoever welcomes one such child in my name welcomes me, and whoever welcomes me welcomes not me but the one who sent me.”
9.30–37: Second passion prediction and discipleship instruction (Mt 17.22–23; 18.1–9; Lk 9.43b–48). See also 8.27–9.1; 10.32–45.

37 : , possible allusion to Mark's community as it carries out Jesus's mission after the resurrection. , Jesus is a specially designated agent of God's kingdom. Cf. Jn 12.44–45. Similar sayings appear in 10.15; Mt 10.40; 18.5; Lk 9.48; 10.16; 18.17.
John said to him, “Teacher, we saw someone casting out demons in your name, and we tried to stop him, because he was not following us.”
But Jesus said, “Do not stop him; for no one who does a deed of power in my name will be able soon afterward to speak evil of me.
39 : , see 5.30; 6.2,14. Jesus's mission establishes a foothold for God's sovereign power; as a result, others wield that restorative power as well.
Whoever is not against us is for us.
For truly I tell you, whoever gives you a cup of water to drink because you bear the name of Christ will by no means lose the reward.
9.38–41: An unknown exorcist (Lk 9.49–50; cf. Num 11.27–29).
“If any of you put a stumbling block before one of these little ones who believe in me, it would be better for you if a great millstone were hung around your neck and you were thrown into the sea.
42 : , see 9.36–37. Mark probably expands the term to include society's weaker members. , used to grind grain and turned by a donkey or mule.
If your hand causes you to stumble, cut it off; it is better for you to enter life maimed than to have two hands and to go to hell, to the unquenchable fire.

43 : , a hyperbolic expression. See also 9.45. 47; Mt 5.30. , lit., “Gehenna,” a valley in Jerusalem associated with pagan rites (see 2 Kings 23.10; Jer 7.31) and used in Jesus's day as a trash heap. Metaphorically understood as a place of eternal punishment. See Mt 5.30; Lk 12.5.
And if your foot causes you to stumble, cut it off; it is better for you to enter life lame than to have two feet and to be thrown into hell.c,d
And if your eye causes you to stumble, tear it out; it is better for you to enter the kingdom of God with one eye than to have two eyes and to be thrown into hell,c
where their worm never dies, and the fire is never quenched.
“For everyone will be salted with fire.
49 : , images that combine to signal the coming judgment (see Isa 66.24; 1 Cor 3.13).
Salt is good; but if salt has lost its saltiness, how can you season it? Have salt in yourselves, and be at peace with one another.”

3.19b–35: Jesus at home (Mt 12.22–32; Lk 11.14–23; 12.10). An intertwined intercalation of two passages, linked by the motif of household. For other examples of this technique, see 5.21–43; 11.12–25[26]; 14.1–11.
8.27–15.47: Jesus's messianic mission: sacrifice.
8.27–10.52: The way of the cross.
9.42–50: Jesus warns of coming judgment (Mt 18.6–9; Lk 17.1–2).
